title: 1  Using Hidden Markov Models for Animal Tracking
text:
1.1 Prerequisites
Hidden Markov Models (HMMs) are commonly used to analyze animal movement data. In many tracking datasets we observe locations over time, but we do not directly observe the behavior of the animal. HMMs let researchers infer hidden behavior states like resting, traveling, or foraging based on movement patterns. This guide explains the basic idea behind fitting an HMM to tracking data using R and interpreting the results.
Before following this guide, users should:
crumbs: 1  Using Hidden Markov Models for Animal Tracking

title: 1  Using Hidden Markov Models for Animal Tracking
text:
Have basic knowledge of R and RStudio
Install the “moveHMM” package
Have tracking data with coordinates and timestamps
Understand basic statistical concepts such as probability distributions
crumbs: 1  Using Hidden Markov Models for Animal Tracking

title: 1  Using Hidden Markov Models for Animal Tracking
text:
1.2 Required Data
To apply an HMM to movement data, the dataset should include:

Animal location coordinates (latitude and longitude)
Timestamps for each observation
Observations ordered in time

From these values we can find:

Step length (distance between consecutive locations)
Turning angle (change in movement direction)

These variables are commonly used as the observed data in animal movement HMMs.
crumbs: 1  Using Hidden Markov Models for Animal Tracking

title: 1  Using Hidden Markov Models for Animal Tracking
text:
1.3 Software Setup
There are multiple R packages that can fit HMMs for movement data. One that is commonly used is the moveHMM package Théo Michelot, Langrock, and Patterson (2016).
To install and load the package and its dependencies:
install.packages("CircStats")
install.packages("moveHMM")
library(moveHMM)
We can then load our library and some of its example data:

library(moveHMM)

Warning: package 'moveHMM' was built under R version 4.3.3


Loading required package: CircStats


Loading required package: MASS


Loading required package: boot

data("elk_data")

head(elk_data)

       ID Easting Northing dist_water
1 elk-115  769928  4992847     200.00
2 elk-115  766875  4997444     600.52
3 elk-115  765949  4998516     561.81
4 elk-115  765938  4998277     550.00
5 elk-115  766275  4998005     302.08
6 elk-115  766368  4998051     213.60
crumbs: 1  Using Hidden Markov Models for Animal Tracking

title: 1  Using Hidden Markov Models for Animal Tracking
text:
1.4 Preparing the Data
After loading the data, the prepData() function in the package can calculate step lengths and turning angles from the data using type = “UTM” since we have Easting/Northing data rather than Latitude/Longitude:

data <- prepData(elk_data, type="UTM", coordNames=c("Easting","Northing"))
plot(data,compact=T)
crumbs: 1  Using Hidden Markov Models for Animal Tracking

title: 1  Using Hidden Markov Models for Animal Tracking
text:
1.5 Fitting the Model
Next, choose the number of hidden states in the model. For example, a basic model might include two states:

Traveling
Foraging

It can then be fit using the fitHMM() function. The function estimates the movement characteristics associated with each hidden state and the probabilities of switching between states:

mu0 <- c(50, 300) # step mean (two parameters: one for each state)
sigma0 <- c(50,200) # step SD
zeromass0 <- c(0.1,0.01) # step zero-mass
stepPar0 <- c(mu0,sigma0,zeromass0)

angleMean0 <- c(pi,0) # angle mean
kappa0 <- c(0.5,2) # angle concentration
anglePar0 <- c(angleMean0,kappa0)

model <- fitHMM(data=data,nbStates=2,stepPar0=stepPar0,
anglePar0=anglePar0)

Notice we chose some initial parameters based on moveHMM’s documentation Theo Michelot, Langrock, and Patterson (2025).
crumbs: 1  Using Hidden Markov Models for Animal Tracking

title: 1  Using Hidden Markov Models for Animal Tracking
text:
1.6 Interpreting the Results
After fitting the model, the output will have estimates for the parameters associated with each state and the probabilities of transitioning between them.
We can also plot our model to view for a more straightforward summary of our results:

plot(model)

Decoding states sequence... DONE












































The plots display the estimated step-length and turning-angle distributions for each hidden state, along with the probabilities of transitioning between states. These visualizations help identify movement patterns associated with different behaviors.
Researchers can examine these results to interpret animal behavior. For example, a state associated with longer step lengths and straighter movement may represent traveling, while shorter step lengths and more turning may be foraging.
crumbs: 1  Using Hidden Markov Models for Animal Tracking

title: 1  Using Hidden Markov Models for Animal Tracking
text:
1.7 Limitations
The results of an HMM depend on assumptions such as the number of states and the distributions used to model the data. Because of this, it is important to check whether the chosen states make sense and to compare alternative specifications if possible.




Michelot, Theo, Roland Langrock, and Toby Patterson. 2025. “moveHMM: An R Package for the Analysis of Animal Movement Data,” October.


Michelot, Théo, Roland Langrock, and Toby A. Patterson. 2016. “moveHMM: An r Package for the Statistical Modelling of Animal Movement Data Using Hidden Markov Models.” Methods in Ecology and Evolution.
crumbs: 1  Using Hidden Markov Models for Animal Tracking

text:

title: Appendix A — Topic
text:
This user guide focuses on the use of hidden Markov models (HMMs) for analyzing animal tracking data. HMMs are commonly used to predict unobserved behaviors, like foraging, traveling, or resting, from observed movement data.
The scope of this guide is limited to a conceptual overview of how HMMs are applied to animal tracking data and how their outputs are interpreted. It assumes basic knowledge in statistics and R, but won’t directly cover the mathematics and processes of HMMs.






NoteComments/suggestions



Ok, I think that’s reasonable. You may want to find an R package that allows you to fit such models without going into the math - it will probably make your life easier to demonstrate how to do the input and interpret the output if you have a specific package that will handle the math details.

title: Appendix B — Needs Assessment
text:
What does someone trying to accomplish your chosen task need help with?
Someone trying to use HMMs for animal tracking data would probably need a way to understand what HMMs and and how to apply them to R
What parts are likely to be tricky?

Understanding hidden states
Choosing the amount of states
Interpreting the output
Preparing the movement data

What resources are already available on this topic that may be helpful? Look for e.g. software vignettes, package documentation, papers about software packages, and so on.

R package documentation like moveHMM or momentuHMM
Help files in R
Package vignettes







Note



I’d expect you to have read the vignettes and identified which ones are helpful for each of the tricky parts.

title: Appendix C — Task Analysis
text:
C.1 Additional Guidance
Here are some questions to guide you through the process of doing a task analysis. You don’t have to specifically answer each one of these (and some may not apply), but they should get you started thinking in the right direction.
What are the prerequisites, for both knowledge and e.g. software setup?
What are the basic requirements for any data that the method is used on? - Generally no missing data - Should be ordered in time
What are the basic components of the task?
What decisions does the user need to be prepared to make on the fly?
What questions should the user ask of the “first draft” of the product? What adjustments may need to be made?
Your check-in should answer these basic questions (and similar concerns that apply more directly to your topic).
Once you’ve completed the check-in, you can use this section to jump-start an introduction/set-up/getting started section in your user guide. This document should remain as an appendix to your main report.
